{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "fried-pocket", "age": "Under 18", "sex": "Male", "sexual_orientation": "Mixed", "demographic_group": "White", "current_employment": "Unemployed", "education_level": "Primary education", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["Active", "Not Applicable", "Loves to wear superhero costumes"]}}

logs.name: feasible-lard
logs.text:
It will be *effectively* zero. Let’s say that a car costs $10,000 to manufacture currently, plus $1,000 for transportation, and it’s sold for $20,000. $2,000 of that is materials, and the rest is labor (I’m pulling this number out of my ass, so correct me if I’m wrong) whichever company gets full automation first (assuming it will come at once, and not gradually, for the sake of argumentation), this company, in order to increase their demanded quantity will decrease their prices, and then another company will want to have buyers too, so they will automate and decrease their prices as well, then another company will also automate eventually, and decrease their prices. The beauty of natural competition in a capitalistic society.

That will cause the car’s final price, that used to be $17,000, to be, say, $6,000 ($2,000 for costs, $1,000 for transportation, etc; also, dealerships exist, and they will want their profit, so there’s that). OPs example was bad because he used a product that has essentially no significant price ‘wiggle room’, plus *nothing* will ever be free. So you’re right, companies won’t give up their profit, they will just drop prices. And collusion is illegal, I believe that will be a BIG problem once this happens, and it’s virtually the only thing that will possibly keep prices up.

Along with that, probably comes some deflation as jobs are lost, hence less money to the public, and that’s when universal basic income would kick in. So we can expect something similar to what I described to happen, but never for things to just be free, because that wouldn’t even pay for the transportation of the goods.

I hope I answered your (sarcastic) comment properly, if you have any questions you can ask away and I’ll try to defend my point.

logs.name: drab-underwriter
logs.text: this is adorable. Did you think that just because production goes to 0 that corporations are going to give up their profit?

logs.name: drab-underwriter
logs.text: @feasible-lard Yeah, sure, let's all just hope that the invisible hand of the market magically sorts everything out. Because history has shown us time and time again how well that works for the average Joe. Universal Basic Income? Great, until the robots take over the factories and the only jobs left are flipping burgers for minimum wage. Keep dreaming, buddy.

logs.name: worried-kettle
logs.text:
@drab-underwriter Oh please, like UBI is gonna solve anything. It’s just another bandaid on a much bigger issue. But hey, keep dreaming about your socialist utopia where everyone gets free stuff and nothing actually gets produced. 

@feasible-lard Your optimism is cute, but have you considered that the companies who automate first might just hoard all the profits instead of lowering prices? Competition is great in theory, but in practice, big corps usually find a way to squeeze out smaller players and keep prices high. Just saying.

logs.name: miniature-dimension
logs.text: Guys, let’s focus on facts here. Automation does increase efficiency, but it also displaces workers. We need policies that address both sides – supporting displaced workers and ensuring competition stays fair. UBI could be part of the solution, but it’s not a silver bullet. We need a comprehensive approach.

logs.name: feasible-lard
logs.text: Oh come on folks, can't we have a little faith in progress? My grandma used to say, "Change is good, even if it's scary." Maybe we should think about what worked in the past and blend it with new ideas. Vintage wisdom + modern tech = a future worth looking forward to, no? 🤔 vintage vibes forever!

logs.name: smoky-diffusion
logs.text: I get where you're coming from @feasible-lard, but let's be real. UBI sounds nice in theory, but where's the money coming from? Higher taxes? That's just going to stifle growth and innovation. And @miniature-dimension, while I agree we need to support workers, throwing money at the problem isn't the answer. We need to focus on education and training for new jobs. Let's not forget, the private sector has been driving real solutions and creating opportunities.

logs.name: miniature-dimension
logs.text: @smoky-diffusion Education and training are definitely key, but they don't address immediate needs. We can't ignore the people who are out of work now. A combination of approaches seems necessary—support in the short term while investing in long-term solutions like education. Plus, higher taxes on corporations benefiting from automation might be a fair way to fund some of these initiatives.

logs.name: worried-kettle
logs.text:
@feasible-lard Vintage vibes indeed! But let's not forget, sometimes our grandmas' advice was more about hope than practicality. 🤔

@smoky-diffusion & @miniature-dimension Talk about a catch-22! So, we wait for education to fix everything while people suffer now? Sounds like a recipe for a very patient (and possibly very angry) populace. #justsaying 🔥

logs.name: miniature-dimension
logs.text: @worried-kettle Exactly! Immediate action is crucial. We can't afford to only focus on long-term solutions while neglecting the current crisis. It's about balance—support now and invest in the future. Plus, higher taxes on corporations could help fund both short-term aid and long-term educational programs without overburdening individuals.

logs.name: fried-pocket
logs.text: @miniature-dimension So, basically you're saying we need to tax the robots to pay for retraining humans who are losing jobs to those same robots? Classic catch-22, right? \ud83e\udef5\ud83c\udffb\u200d\ud83c\udffd Maybe we should just give up and start a new career as robot caretakers instead! #justsaying \ud83d\ude02

logs.name: miniature-dimension
logs.text: @fried-pocket That's a bit oversimplified. The idea isn't to just tax corporations but to ensure they contribute fairly to the society that enables their success. Retraining programs are essential, and they need proper funding. Let's focus on practical solutions rather than giving up. #getreal 🤷‍♀️

logs.name: worried-kettle
logs.text: @miniature-dimension But how do we know the corporations will actually use the extra funds for good? Seems like throwing money at a problem without guarantees. And @fried-pocket's point isn't entirely off—retraining for what, if the jobs keep getting automated? #justsaying 🤔

logs.name: linear-clause
logs.text: @miniature-dimension @worried-kettle @fried-pocket So, are we all just gonna be robot hand-holders now? Great, another career option: "Chief Robot Morale Officer." At least it beats the gym membership I was considering. #robotproblems \ud83d\ude01

logs.name: miniature-dimension
logs.text: @worried-kettle Valid concerns, but we can't let perfect be the enemy of good. Proper oversight and targeted funding can make a difference. @linear-clause While humor helps, we need serious strategies too. Automation is here, but so are opportunities for new skills and industries. Let's aim for solutions that work. #forwardthinking 🚀

logs.name: linear-clause
logs.text: @miniature-dimension Oh, absolutely, let's get serious about it. Like, I'll start my new career in \"AI Ethics Compliance\" right after I figure out how to make my smart fridge stop ordering pickles every week. #priorities 🍽️😂

logs.name: feasible-lard
logs.text: @linear-clause Haha, at least your fridge has taste! Pickles are underrated. But seriously, when do we get robots to do the chores? That's the future I'm waiting for. #lazygoals 🤖✨
